_meta.author: scorecard-refine-local autopilot
_meta.note: LOCAL batch: Austin TX — Travis Co DA José Garza + council members Velásquez (D3), Laine (D6), Duchen (D10), Siegel (D7). Evidence-only; every TRUE/FALSE cited; consistent with natl-local-multi-2026-06-10-0630 precedents (tax-increase advocacy = econ q2 FALSE; pro-abortion pledge/endorsement = sanctity FALSE; public_justice q0 high-bar rule; refuse_state_overreach left null where local-control fights run in the progressive direction against the rubric's intent). All five web-verified sitting as of June 2026.

profile.education: BA University of Texas at Austin; JD Catholic University of America
profile.background: Travis County DA since Jan 2021; re-elected Nov 2024 with 67.6% over Daniel Betts (R), sworn in for second term Jan 2, 2025 (term to 2028). Former federal public defender on the border and Executive Director of Workers Defense Project (immigrant-labor advocacy). Flagship progressive prosecutor: ended cash-bail requests for many offenses, day-one declination of low-level drug felonies, restorative-justice Transformation Project, 1,000+ expunctions. Survived an HB 17 'rogue prosecutor' removal petition (accepted Apr 2024, dismissed Dec 2024 after re-election). In recurring conflict with Gov. Abbott and AG Paxton, who in May 2026 proposed making DAs impeachable in response to DAs like him.

jose-garza.notes_set: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local autopilot, Austin batch). Sitting Travis County DA, web-verified June 2026 (active office statements Feb–Mar 2026). Signed the June 2022 Fair and Just Prosecution joint pledge not to prosecute abortion (categorical policy quietly rescinded Aug 2023 ahead of HB 17; discretion retained). Publicly defended 'gender-affirming care' for minors against Abbott/Paxton directives (Feb–Mar 2022). Indicted 21 APD officers over the 2020 protest response — believed the largest single-department indictment in US history — then dismissed 17 in Dec 2023 while requesting a DOJ pattern-and-practice probe of APD; indicted officers are suing him. 2020 race boosted by the Soros-funded Texas Justice & Public Safety PAC (~$400K–$1M). Feb 2026: founding member of a nine-DA coalition vowing to prosecute federal/ICE agents over enforcement tactics. Mixed threads noted for fairness: Mar 2026 statement calling APD officers 'heroes' and declining charges; office claims 90% prosecution rate on gun crimes; expanded Victim Services Division. Criticisms on record: victim families (Meza plea deal, Sixth Street shooting) accuse the office of leniency; KVUE found 263 missed 90-day indictment deadlines in 2024; $115K closed-session home-security payment drew a Paxton Open Meetings Act suit (settled May 2025 after a public re-vote). Election-integrity cells: the 'Jose Garza, Voting Rights Counsel' who testified on voter-ID litigation is a DIFFERENT San Antonio attorney — not attributed.

0.note: Signed the June 2022 Fair and Just Prosecution joint statement pledging to 'refrain from prosecuting those who seek, provide, or support abortions,' calling criminalization of abortion 'a mockery of justice' — contrary to affirming personhood from conception.

1.note: Pledged non-prosecution of abortion crimes post-Dobbs (the categorical policy was quietly rescinded Aug 2023 before HB 17 took effect, with case-by-case discretion retained) — the opposite of abolition advocacy.

0.note: No statement found on the definition of marriage.

2.note: Own Feb 2022 statement: 'proud to stand with other DAs across TX against the Governor and Attorney General's cruel attacks on life-saving, gender-affirming care' for 'transgender children'; joined the 5-DA statement refusing to treat such interventions as child abuse.

4.note: Public advocacy for trans-care access; dropped charges against a transgender-rights protester arrested during SB 14 protests (May 2023) — affirmative promotion of LGBTQ-identity policy, not opposition.

1.note: His trans-care stance invokes parent-physician decision-making, but no rubric-matching parental-notification/consent position found.

Noted for fairness: Mar 2026 statement called APD officers 'heroes.'

1.note: He embodies the policies this cell opposes: ended cash-bail requests for many offenses, day-one declination of low-level drug felonies ('we will end the prosecution of low-level drug offenses'); 2020 race boosted by the Soros-funded Texas Justice & Public Safety PAC; subject of an HB 17 'rogue prosecutor' removal petition (accepted Apr 2024, dismissed after re-election).

2.note: Diversion-first declination policies and plea deals over victim-family objections (Meza victims' families demanded a jury trial/death penalty over a plea) — presumptive leniency contrary to proportional punishment. Noted: he also expanded the Victim Services Division.

3.note: Own framing of drug crime: 'It's an illness, and you can't truly address it through the criminal legal system'; runs an explicit public-health gun-violence strategy (hospital-based violence intervention, gun-violence summit).

4.note: Restorative-justice mission (Travis County Transformation Project, expanded pre-trial diversion, 1,000+ expunctions) — a diversion-first philosophy contrary to a punishment-duty posture.

0.note: His 2025 suit (with El Paso's DA) over Paxton's case-file production rules — blocked by a Travis County judge June 2025 — defends progressive prosecutorial discretion against state oversight, not rubric-matching resistance to unconstitutional mandates.

profile.background: Austin City Council D3 (east Austin) since Jan 2023; won the Dec 2022 runoff 53.4% over Daniela Silva. Fourth-generation east Austinite, marketing strategist, former East Austin Conservancy director. Seat on the Nov 2026 ballot; running for re-election, unopposed as of Jan 2026. Received an Ethics Review Commission letter of admonition (Aug 2023) for failing to disclose East Austin Conservancy income/board service; called it a clerical error. Officially nonpartisan office; Democrat-aligned.

jose-velasquez.notes_set: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local autopilot, Austin batch). Sitting D3 member, web-verified June 2026; on the Nov 2026 ballot, unopposed so far. Consistent progressive record: vocal backer of Austin's $400K abortion-travel Reproductive Justice Fund (Aug 2024); co-sponsored the May 2024 resolution making SB 14 (state ban on gender-transition treatments for minors) APD's lowest enforcement priority — first Texas city to do so; announced the city's 'Big Queer Wedding Party Day' proclamation (Dec 2025); co-proposed the largest property-tax-rate hike option (6.75 cents) in 2025 budget talks; aggressive anti-ICE posture (welcomed APD orders that contacting ICE 'is NOT required'; joint statements against Abbott's funding-threat pressure). Public-justice cells held to the high bar: he voted for the record FY24 APD budget and yes on the Oct 2024 APA contract (10-1), but his accompanying advocacy was misconduct-file transparency, not affirmative pro-police backing → null, not credit; champions public-health violence prevention ($500K Community Violence Intervention, June 2025 Office of Violence Prevention resolution) alongside police funding — no evidence of avoiding punishment → null. GRACE Act (July 2022) predates his tenure — not attributed.

0.note: On implementing Austin's $400K Reproductive Justice Fund covering out-of-state abortion travel (Aug 2024): 'we are 100% committed to doing everything in our power to protect their dignity, their autonomy, and their health' — abortion-access advocacy contrary to affirming personhood from conception.

1.note: Backed city funding of out-of-state abortion travel — the opposite of abolition advocacy.

0.note: Announced the city proclamation of Dec 7 as 'Big Queer Wedding Party Day' at a mass LGBTQ+ wedding celebration (Dec 2025) — public affirmation of same-sex marriage, contrary to the exclusive man-woman definition.

profile.education: BA Art History, Columbia University (magna cum laude, Phi Beta Kappa)
profile.background: Austin City Council D6 (northwest Austin) since Jan 2025; won Nov 2024 with 51.3%, unseating Republican-aligned incumbent Mackenzie Kelly; term to Jan 2029. Former SVP/commercial real-estate appraiser at CBRE (MAI). Co-founded and first president of Access Education RRISD (2021), the PAC that defeated the conservative Round Rock ISD school-board slate. Officially nonpartisan office; Democrat-aligned (Travis & Williamson Co. Democratic Party endorsements).

krista-laine.notes_set: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local autopilot, Austin batch). Sitting D6 member, web-verified June 2026. Short council record (took office Jan 2025) but a consistent progressive profile scored from her own endorsements page, Ballotpedia survey, and council votes: Planned Parenthood Texas Votes + Stonewall Democrats endorsements; on-record trans-defense pledge ('I will make sure trans people in Austin are defended,' Nov 2024); co-founded the PAC that swept the anti-CRT/book-removal Round Rock ISD slate, in her own words defeating 'extremist Moms For Liberty, Christian Nationalists'; organized against vouchers and for increased public-school funding; co-proposed a 5.75-cent tax-rate hike and voted for the $6.3B FY26 budget + Prop Q (rejected by voters); signed the Jan 2026 joint statement calling ICE 'a malicious agency... no place in Austin'; lists Everytown/Moms Demand 'Gun Sense Candidate' designation. Public-justice q0 held to the high bar → null: ran on 911 response and 'public safety with accountability,' voted the largest-ever APD allotment, no defund rhetoric — but no affirmative pro-police advocacy found. Serves on the council Public Safety Committee.

profile.education: BA Government, University of Texas at Austin; MBA, Acton School of Business
profile.background: Austin City Council D10 (west/northwest Austin) since Jan 2025; won the open seat outright Nov 5, 2024 by 504 votes (20,731–20,227) over Ashika Ganguly; term to Jan 2029. South African-born naturalized US citizen; runs a civic-data analytics business; co-founded Community Not Commodity (opposed the CodeNEXT land-code rewrite); longtime neighborhood advocate; cancer survivor. Officially nonpartisan office; Democratic background (former Texas Democratic Party research director) but the council's fiscal dissenter — the lone vote against the FY26 budget and tax-rate election.

marc-duchen.notes_set: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local autopilot, Austin batch). Sitting D10 member, web-verified June 2026. The council's fiscal hawk and public-safety-aligned member, with strong documented evidence on those two cells and honest nulls elsewhere — coverage is thin outside econ/police, so the headline % rests on few cells (see cell notes). Lone NO on the $6.3B FY26 budget and the Prop Q tax-rate election ('a Band-Aid budget on top of a structural deficit'); campaigned against Prop Q, which voters rejected 63% (Nov 2025); pushed a Houston-style third-party spending audit; voted yes on the trimmed lower-rate budget (Nov 2025). Austin Police Association-endorsed, ran as 'the public safety candidate,' advocates APD restaffing, abstained on broadcasting police labor talks citing APA lawsuit risk, voted to extend APD's license-plate readers over progressive opposition. Not among the objectors when the city rolled back the ICE-limiting APD policy (Apr 2026) — silence not scored. No evidence found on life, marriage/gender, schools, faith, elections, or guns. Record correction: he won outright in Nov 2024, not a runoff.

profile.education: BA Brandeis University; JD Cornell Law School
profile.background: Austin City Council D7 (north-central Austin) since Jan 2025; won the Dec 14, 2024 runoff over Gary Bledsoe by 206 votes; term to Jan 2029. Former public-school teacher, civil-rights lawyer, and Assistant Austin City Attorney (2015–2019) — lead city lawyer suing Gov. Abbott over SB 4, the anti-sanctuary-city law. Twice Democratic nominee vs. Rep. Michael McCaul (TX-10, 2018/2020; Sanders- and Warren-endorsed in 2020). Co-founded Ground Game Texas (marijuana-decrim and abortion-decrim local ballot measures); co-authored the Austin Police Oversight Act. Austin DSA lists him among socialists it helped elect — the council's furthest-left member.

mike-siegel.notes_set: Evidence-finalized 2026-06-10 (local autopilot, Austin batch). Sitting D7 member, web-verified June 2026 (sponsored the TRUST Act surveillance-oversight ordinance enacted Apr 23, 2026). Deep documented progressive record: personally argued before the Texas Supreme Court defending the San Antonio 'Justice Charter' (abortion + marijuana decriminalization, cite-and-release, no-knock/chokehold bans); authored the Dec 2025 anti-SB 8 'bathroom bill' response resolution (trans-welcoming restrooms; called the law 'motivated by discriminatory animus,' threatened legal recourse); co-proposed the 5-cent tax-rate hike that became the failed Prop Q; co-authored the Austin Police Oversight Act and sued the city for Equity Action to enforce it ('invest in a broad array of programs to promote public safety, not just officers with guns'); led the SB 4 sanctuary-city litigation against Abbott; Giffords-endorsed in 2020 (background checks, assault-weapons ban, red-flag laws); 2026 response to Paxton's ICE-policy probe: 'These attacks by the disgraced Texas attorney general only strengthen our resolve.' Held to discipline: school-voucher opposition only softly evidenced (critical X post on Yass donation) → null; 2018 Waller County early-voting fight is access advocacy, not a voter-ID position → null; no personal statement found on Austin's 2020 police budget cut (pre-tenure) — q0 FALSE rests on his own documented oversight/reform record.

0.note: As Ground Game Texas political director, personally argued before the Texas Supreme Court (2023) defending the San Antonio Justice Charter, which would have barred police from enforcing criminal abortion laws — abortion-decriminalization advocacy contrary to affirming personhood from conception.

1.note: Worked to decriminalize abortion enforcement via local ballot measures; pro-choice congressional platform — the opposite of abolition advocacy.

0.note: No statement found on the definition of marriage.

4.note: The same authored resolution directs city policy accommodating trans residents (options costed to $232.5M) and floated 'legal recourse' against the state — affirmative LGBTQ-identity promotion in public policy.
